text: The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum below the infundibulopelvic ligament to mobilize the ureter laterally. The common iliac artery is visible, bifurcating into the external and internal iliac arteries. The ureter is identified crossing cranially to this bifurcation and is dissected away from its attachment to the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon mobilizes the ureter along its entire length, lateralizing it away from the nodular attachment. This step is crucial for safely opening the Obakayashi space and dissecting the pararectal area without risking trauma to the ureter.
A window is created under the ureter, which is then extended using blunt traction to lateralize the ureter away from the borders of the uterus and vagina.

The surgeon performs further lateralization of the ureter, extending dissection to the level of the ureteric tunnel or corneal space. The ureter is visibly mobilized, with the retroperitoneum lifted to shift the rectum and pararectal space medially. The ureter is gently dissected away laterally, freeing it from lateral attachments.

The surgeon opens the Obakayashi space, revealing the rectum plastered to the posterior cervix. The rectum is dissected away from the nodule by going below its level, separating the rectum from the nodular attachment.

The surgeon uses blunt traction to dissect the rectum away from its nodular attachment in the deep pararectal space. Once separated cranially and caudally, options include using scissors to cut the nodule or applying gentle coagulation current to further separate the rectum. If the nodule is thick, a discoid resection of the rectum may be necessary.

The surgeon addresses a dense point attachment of the rectum, performing skinning of the rectal musculature by opening the longitudinal muscles while leaving the circular muscles intact. The rectum is then mobilized away from the posterior vaginal wall, extending the separation to the level of the anorectal junction without requiring suturing.
